coredata.dc:title: Continuous response fluorescence sensor for three small molecules based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots from prunus lannesiana and their logic gate operation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Zicheng
Liu, Xuerui
Yu, Haiyu
Hou, Faju
Gao, Shanmin
Zhong, Linlin
Xu, Hui
Yu, Yang
Meng, Junli
Wang, Ruru
coredata.dc:description: In this study, an environmentally friendly and water-soluble nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with quantum yield (QY) of 8.59% were prepared by one-step hydrothermal synthesis without any chemical reagent using the leaves of prunus lannesiana as precursors. The properties and quality of N-CQDs were investigated by Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectroscopy, infrared spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, zeta potential, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. The fluorescence of the prepared N-CQDs can be quenched by Fe3+ through the synergistic effect of the formation of non-fluorescent complex and internal filtration effect (IFE) between Fe3+ and N-CQDs. And the quenched fluorescence can be “turned on” after adding ascorbic acid (AA) because Fe3+ can be released from the surface of N-CQDs through the redox reaction between AA and Fe3+. While the restored fluorescence can be “turned off” again by hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) due to the re-oxidation of Fe2+ to Fe3+. So, the three inputs “logic gate” is achieved and the “on-off-on-off” continuous response fluorescence sensor is formed, which can be applied for the continuous detection of Fe3+, AA and H2O2 with the linear range of 40–260 μM, 10–200 μM and 40–140 μM, respectively. Finally, the sensor was successfully applied to determine Fe3+, AA and H2O2 in real samples with the satisfactory recoveries (95.35%-104.10%) and repeatability (relative standard deviation (RSD) ≤ 1.68%). The continuous response fluorescence sensor prepared by simple green synthesis route has the characteristics of fast response, acceptable sensitivity and good selectivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)
Fe3+
Ascorbic acid (AA)
Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2)
Continuous response fluorescence sensor
Logic gate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanism of continuous response fluorescent sensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Shandong Province Department of Science and Technology

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HR-TEM image, (b) Size distribution of N-CQDs and (c) Lattice fringes image of a single N-CQDs.
(a) FT-IR spectrum, (b) Full-scan XPS spectrum, (c) C 1s XPS, (d) N 1s XPS, (e) O 1s XPS and (f) Zeta potential of N-CQDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of N-CQDs. (b) The emission spectra of N-CQDs under different excitation wavelengths. (c) Effect of pH, (d) NaCl concentration and (e) Storage time on the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs.
The fluorescence intensity changes of N-CQDs, N-CQDs/Fe3+, N-CQDs/Fe3+/AA and N-CQDs/Fe3+/AA/H2O2 system.

(a) Fluorescence quenching effect at 430 nm in the presence of 260 μM different ions. (b) Effect of pH, (c) Reaction time and (d) N-CQDs concentration on the fluorescence quenching effect of N-CQDs/Fe3+ system. (e) Fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs/Fe3+ system in various concentration of Fe3+ (0–300 μM). (f) F vs Fe3+ relationship (the inset shows the linear detection range).

(a) Effect of reaction time on the fluorescence quenching effect of N-CQDs/Fe3+/AA system. (b) Fluorescence quenching effect at 430 nm in the presence of 200 μM different amino acids, vitamins, reducing agents. (c) Fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs/Fe3+/AA system in various concentration of AA (0–250 μM). (d) F vs AA relationship (the inset shows the linear detection range).
Photographic image of letters using N-CQDs as fluorescent ink under (a) sunlight and (b) ultraviolet illumination.
Synthesis of N-CQDs and its sensing mechanism for Fe3+, AA and H2O2.
Logic gate scheme of the continuous response fluorescence sensor.

head.ce:title: Continuous response fluorescence sensor for three small molecules based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots from prunus lannesiana and their logic gate operation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Ludong University, Yantai 264025, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Ludong University, Yantai 264025, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Qiuzhen College, Huzhou University, Huzhou 313000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Qiuzhen College, Huzhou University, Huzhou 313000, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Zicheng Guo and Xuerui Liu contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of N-CQDs and its sensing mechanism for Fe3+, AA and H2O2.

In this study, an environmentally friendly and water-soluble nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with quantum yield (QY) of 8.59% were prepared by one-step hydrothermal synthesis without any chemical reagent using the leaves of prunus lannesiana as precursors. The properties and quality of N-CQDs were investigated by Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectroscopy, infrared spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, zeta potential, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. The fluorescence of the prepared N-CQDs can be quenched by Fe3+ through the synergistic effect of the formation of non-fluorescent complex and internal filtration effect (IFE) between Fe3+ and N-CQDs. And the quenched fluorescence can be “turned on” after adding ascorbic acid (AA) because Fe3+ can be released from the surface of N-CQDs through the redox reaction between AA and Fe3+. While the restored fluorescence can be “turned off” again by hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) due to the re-oxidation of Fe2+ to Fe3+. So, the three inputs “logic gate” is achieved and the “on-off-on-off” continuous response fluorescence sensor is formed, which can be applied for the continuous detection of Fe3+, AA and H2O2 with the linear range of 40–260 μM, 10–200 μM and 40–140 μM, respectively. Finally, the sensor was successfully applied to determine Fe3+, AA and H2O2 in real samples with the satisfactory recoveries (95.35%-104.10%) and repeatability (relative standard deviation (RSD) ≤ 1.68%). The continuous response fluorescence sensor prepared by simple green synthesis route has the characteristics of fast response, acceptable sensitivity and good selectivity.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new type of carbon nanomaterials with size less than 10 nm developed in recent years. Since the accidental discovery by Xu et al. in 2004 [1], they have been widely used for waste water treatment [2], bio-imaging [3], chemical sensing [4,5], photocatalysis [6–8] and metal ion detection [9] due to their good safety, low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, photoluminescence and high water solubility. A variety of methods for the preparation of CQDs have been reported, such as chemical oxidation [10], electrochemical method [11], hydrothermal method [12], microwave-assisted method [13] and pyrolysis method [14]. Among them, hydrothermal method has been favored by researchers due to the advantages of low cost, simple operation and environmental friendliness. On the other hand, most of the reported CQDs exhibit short fluorescence lifetime and poor selectivity in detection, so their applications are partially restricted. As far as we know, non-metallic elements doping (such as N, P, S and B) can provide more abundant active sites and functional groups on the surface of CQDs and can expand the scope of application [15–19]. For example, Lu et al. prepared nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) using watermelon juice as the precursor for cell imaging, detection of Fe3+ and cysteine [20]. Although N-CQDs can be customized by modifying CQDs surfaces using nitrogen-containing functional molecules, the surface modification process is usually complex and time consuming. As reported, N-CQDs can also be prepared directly by hydrothermal treatment of nitrogen-containing raw materials. For example, Chinese yams, orange juice and carrot juice have been used as raw materials to prepare N-CQDs [21–23]. In addition, the nitrogen doping process can make CQDs obtain other surface properties [24–26], such as optical and catalytic activities. Although some progress has been made so far, more research needs to be done to prepare N-CQDs with multi-functional surface properties from abundant biomass.

Currently, with the increase of environmental protection efforts, iron element has attracted wide attention. Iron is an essential trace element of human body and the core part of hemoglobin, which plays an important role in the transmission of oxygen and participating in human metabolic activities. It is an indispensable substance in many physiological processes of human body. However, excessive iron may induce the occurrence and development of tumors. The iron deficiency can lead to heme formation disorders, which in turn cause red blood cell formation disorders and iron-deficiency anemia [27,28]. Therefore, the determination of iron ion or ferrous ion content is of great significance. In recent years, the detection of iron element based on CQDs [29–31] and Fenton reaction [32–34] have been reported. Ascorbic acid (AA) has antioxidant properties, which can effectively reduce the incidence rate of cancer [35]. It also can reduce Fe3+ to Fe2+, promoting the absorption of iron in the body [36]. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is a common oxidant and an active substance, which plays an important regulatory role in various biological processes in the body [37,38]. Fe3+, AA and H2O2 are important to the human body, and their deficiency or excess can lead to serious diseases. Therefore, it is still very practical to develop a simple, rapid and highly sensitive fluorescence sensing method for the determination of Fe3+, AA and H2O2 in food analysis and environmental sanitation detection fields.

In this study, a new N-CQDs with good biocompatibility were prepared by one-step hydrothermal treatment using the leaves of prunus lannesiana as both carbon source and nitrogen source. An acceptably sensitive “on-off” fluorescence nano sensor based on the quenching effect (F/F0, F0 is the initial fluorescence intensity, and F is the fluorescence intensity after adding the analyte) between the prepared N-CQDs and the Fe3+ was established for the determination of Fe3+. At the same time, it was found that AA could “turn on” the fluorescence sensor and the content of AA in samples could be quantitatively determined by this “on” effect. And more interestingly, the fluorescence intensity was quenched again after adding H2O2 and “turn off” effect was formed. So as a model, the “on-off-on-off” continuous response fluorescence sensor and the three inputs “logic gate” were obtained. Figure Scheme 1 Sensing method for the continuous determination of Fe3+, AA and H2O2 with acceptable sensitivity and good selectivity was established.

The leaves of prunus lannesiana were picked on campus (Yantai, China). All analytical pure reagents were purchased from commercial channels and used as received. Ultrapure water was used throughout all the experiment.

Fluorescence spectra were determined on the LS-55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, America). High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were recorded on a Talos F200X G2 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra were obtained with a UV-2550 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were determined on a MAGNA550 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). The zeta potential was recorded on a Nano-ZS90 instrument (Malvern Zetasizer, Britain). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were measured by an ESCALAB Xi+ photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America).

N-CQDs were prepared by one-step hydrothermal treatment. 2 g dry powdered leaves of prunus lannesiana and 25 mL ultrapure water were sealed into a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 6 h. After the reaction, the autoclave was cooled down to ambient temperature naturally. The resulted solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 5 min to remove big particles. The obtained supernatant was then dialyzed against ultrapure water for 24 h to afford aqueous solution of N-CQDs. Finally, the brown solution was stored in the refrigerator at 4 °C.

Quinine sulfate (QY ~ 54%, in 0.1 M H2SO4) was chosen as the reference to determine the QY of N-CQDs. The QY of N-CQDs in water was calculated using the following equation:
QY
x
=
QY
std
×
A
std
/
A
x
×
I
x
/
I
std
×
(
η
x
/
η
std
)
2

In this equation, “x” and “std” represent N-CQDs and quinine sulfate, respectively. “A” is the absorbance and “I” stands for the integral area of the fluorescence peak. In addition, “η” refers to the refractive index of solvent.

Typically, 8.0 µL N-CQDs solution (0.021 mg/mL) was added to a cuvette, then a series of different final concentration of Fe3+ (40–260 µM) was added. The final volume of the solution was added to 3.0 mL with HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH = 4.0). The mixture was set for 30 s before measuring fluorescence emission spectrum (λex = 340 nm) at room temperature. In addition, in order to determine the selectivity of the proposed sensors, Fe3+ was replaced by other ions with the final concentration of 260 μM. All experiments were repeated three times.

In a typical analysis for the detection of AA, 8.0 µL N-CQDs solution (0.021 mg/mL) and Fe3+ ions (260 μM) were added to a cuvette. Subsequently, different final concentrations of AA (10–200 µM) were gradually added and the final volume of the solution was added to 3.0 mL with HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH = 4.0). The fluorescence emission spectrum of the mixture was determined at room temperature (λex = 340 nm) after setting for 30 s.

Typically, N-CQDs solution (8.0 µL, 0.021 mg/mL), Fe3+ ions (260 μM) and AA (200 µM) were added to a cuvette in turn. Then, different final concentrations of H2O2 (40–140 µM) were gradually added and the final volume of the solution was added to 3.0 mL with HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH = 4.0). The fluorescence emission spectrum of the mixture was determined at room temperature (λex = 340 nm) after setting for 60 s.

The typical HRTEM image of the prepared N-CQDs was illustrated in Fig. 1 a, the N-CQDs were well-dispersed with a diameter of about 5.38 ± 1.36 nm (Fig. 1b), and the prepared N-CQDs exhibited lattice fringes of 0.21 nm, consistent with the (100) facet of graphite carbon (Fig. 1c) [39,40]. Functional groups of N-CQDs were measured by FTIR. As illustrated in Fig. 2 a, the peak at 1070 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of C-O bond [41] Two peaks at 1390 cm−1 and 1600 cm−1 were due to the stretching vibration of C-N and C = C bonds [42]. Another obvious peak at 3390 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of O-H and N-H bonds [42]. XPS spectra, as shown in Fig. 2b, was further determined the elemental composition of N-CQDs. Three distinct peaks in the XPS spectra of N-CQDs were obtained regarding to C 1 s (285 eV), N 1 s (400 eV) and O 1 s (531 eV). Four peaks of C-C/C = C (284.3 eV), C = N (286.7 eV), C-O (285.7 eV) and C = O (287.8 eV) were acquired from the C 1 s peaks (Fig. 2c). Two obvious peaks of -C-N (399.4 eV) and -N-H (400.1 eV) were divided from the N 1 s peak (Fig. 2d). Three peaks of *O = C-OH (532.1 eV), –COOH/C = O-C (531.2 eV) and C = O (532.8 eV) were deconvoluted from the O 1 s peak (Fig. 2e) [43–45]. All these results from the FTIR and XPS demonstrated that there were a good deal of hydroxyl and carbonyl groups on the surface of N-CQDs. In addition, the zeta potential (Fig. 2f) of N-CQDs was −12.2 mV, which indicated once again that there was a certain amount of negatively charged groups such as –COOH and –OH on the surface of N-CQDs.

UV–visabsorption spectrum, fluorescence excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of N-CQDs were illustrated in Fig. 3 a, an obvious absorption band at 276 nm appeared, which can be ascribed to the n-π transition of C = O bond [46]. Interestingly, the emission wavelength of N-CQDs was dependent on the excitation, which could be as a result of the surface state defects (Fig. 3b) [47]. When the excitation wavelength was set as 340 nm, the maximum fluorescence emission was generated at 430 nm (Fig. 3a). In addition, quinine sulfate solution (QY ~ 54%, in 0.1 M H2SO4) was selected as a reference, and the QY of prepared N-CQDs in aqueous solution was calculated to be 8.59%.

For practical application of N-CQDs, the high fluorescence stability in harsh environment is of great significance. The effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity was first examined. As shown in Fig. 3c, the experimental results showed that the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs solution was relatively stable as the pH of solution increased from 3.0 to 10.0. In addition, the effect of high ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs was also studied. As shown in Fig. 3d, the fluorescence intensity remained stable when the concentration of NaCl solution increased from 0.0 to 1.0 M. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs had almost no change after 10 days of storage at room temperature (Fig. 3e). Therefore, the N-CQDs possess an excellent high optical-stability in different experimental conditions, which provides a favorable platform for biosensing and environmental applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of continuous response fluorescent sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The possible fluorescence quenching mechanism by Fe3+ was shown in Fig. S1. As shown in Fig. S1a, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-CQDs changed significantly after the addition of Fe3+, which clearly indicated that the surface state of N-CQDs was modified due to the combination with Fe3+ ions [48]. Meanwhile, with the addition of Fe3+, the zeta potential of N-CQDs aqueous solution shifted sharply from − 12.2 mV to + 4.3 mV (Fig. S1b), which may be caused by the consumption of negative charge due to the combination of Fe3+ and carboxyl groups or hydroxyl groups on the surface of N-CQDs [49]. As displayed in Fig. S1c, the fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs (4.80 ns) was similar to that of N-CQDs/Fe3+ system (4.96 ns). The result showed that the fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs was largely controlled by static quenching in the presence of Fe3+, which was mainly due to the formation of non-fluorescent complex between Fe3+ and N-CQDs [49]. All these results suggest that the addition of Fe3+ can induce the formation of N-CQDs/Fe3+ complexes. In addition, as shown in Fig. S1d, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Fe3+ partially overlaps with the fluorescence excitation spectrum of N-CQDs, indicating that internal filtration effect (IFE) occurs when Fe3+ coexists with N-CQDs [50]. Therefore, we speculate that the quenching mechanism is the synergistic effect of the formation of non-fluorescent complex and IFE [50]. When AA is added, the fluorescence of N-CQDs can be sensitively “turned on” through the oxidation–reduction between AA and Fe3+ since the produced Fe2+ has much lower coordination capacity to N-CQDs. The fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs can be restored in this step. Nevertheless, Fe2+ is oxidized to Fe3+ again after the addition of H2O2, which leads to the quenched fluorescence intensity. And the “turn off” effect is formed again (Fig. 4 ). The “on-off-on-off” continuous response fluorescence sensor is formed and can be applied to the detection of Fe3+, AA and H2O2.

High selectivity is very important for an excellent sensing system, so comparative experiment was carried out. Various environmentally relevant metal ions and ion with similar color to Fe3+, including Pb2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Ba2+, Na+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Li+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Co2+, K+, Fe3+ and CrO4 2- were respectively added into N-CQDs solution with the concentration of 260 μM under the same conditions. As depicted in Fig. 5 a and Fig. S2a, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs was obviously quenched by Fe3+, while the fluorescence quenching effect of other metal ions and CrO4 2- was basically negligible, suggesting that the N-CQDs have high selectivity towards Fe3+.

In order to achieve high sensitivity, some other experimental conditions were also optimized before the detection of Fe3+, such as the concentration of N-CQDs, pH and reaction time. As shown in Fig. 5b, the experimental results showed that the maximum fluorescence quenching rate of N-CQDs by Fe3+ was obtained in the range of pH 3.0–5.0. Therefore, pH 4.0 (0.05 M, HAc-NaAc) was selected in further study. Fig. 5c showed that the reaction between N-CQDs and Fe3+ could be balanced quickly, and the quenching interaction could be completed within 30 s, so 30 s was chosen as the reaction time of the subsequent experiment. Besides, the concentration of N-CQDs was one of the important factors affecting the quenching effect. As shown in Fig. 5d, the quenching effect of the system reached maximum when the concentration of N-CQDs was 0.021 mg/mL. So, the optimal concentration of N-CQDs was 0.021 mg/mL.

Under the optimum experimental conditions, a series of Fe3+ with different concentrations were added to the N-CQDs solution. The emission intensity of the N-CQDs solution gradually decreases (Fig. 5e), which means that the introduction of Fe3+ can effectively quench the fluorescence of N-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 5f, there is a good linearity (R2 = 0.981) between the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs and the concentrations of Fe3+ in the range of 40–260 μM with the regression equation of F = -1.04 CFe3+ (μM) + 539.19. The calculated detection limit (S/N = 3) is 1.60 μM, suggesting that N-CQDs can be used as a highly efficient fluorescent probe for the detection of Fe3+.

Based on the fact that AA can reduce Fe3+ to Fe2+, and Fe2+ has no quenching effect on the fluorescence of N-CQDs, the probe of N-CQDs/Fe3+ system for another analytical application of AA was also investigated. The experimental results showed that the quenched fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs/Fe3+ system could be gradually restored after addition of AA (Fig. 4), and the reaction could be balanced quickly (Fig. 6 a). In addition, to check the selectivity of N-CQDs/Fe3+ system for AA, the fluorescence intensity of the system was measured after adding various amino acids vitamins and reducing agents, including AA, nicotinic acid (NA), taurine (TA), L-cysteine (Cys), DL-homocysteine (Hcy), L-glutathione (GSH), dopamine (DA), glucose (Glu), hydroxylamine hydrochloride (HONH3Cl), Sn2+, citric acid (C6H8O7) and oxalic acid (H2C2O4) with the concentration of 200 μM. The experimental results showed that the quenched fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs/Fe3+ system could be restored by AA and could also be partially restored by other reducing agents (Fig. 6b and Fig. S2b), so the presence of reducing agents should be avoided in the measurement. As shown in Fig. 6c, a series of AA with different concentrations were added to the N-CQDs/Fe3+ system, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs/Fe3+ system gradually increased. The linear range for AA is 10–200 μM (Fig. 6d) with the regression equation of F = 1.93 CAA (μM) + 267.91 (inset in Fig. 6d, R2 = 0.998) and the detection limit is calculated to be 2.26 μM (S/N = 3). This indicates that N-CQDs/Fe3+ system can be constructed for the detection of AA.

Based on the fact that H2O2 can re-oxidize Fe2+ to Fe3+, the probe of N-CQDs/Fe3+/AA system for the determination of H2O2 was also studied. As shown in Fig. S3a, the reaction could reach equilibrium within 60 s and remain stable for a period of time. In order to test the selectivity of the system, various common metal ions and oxidizing agents, including NH4 +, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Zn2+, KMnO4, K2Cr2O7, HNO3, H2SO4, HCl, NaNO2, potassium persulfate (K2S2O8), and H2O2 were respectively added into the N-CQDs/Fe3+/AA system with the concentration of 140 μM under the same conditions (Fig. S3b and Fig. S2c). The experimental results showed that KMnO4, K2Cr2O7 and K2S2O8 had certain interference and NaNO2 had a relatively large interference because of their high oxidation capacity. The presence of oxidizing agents should be avoided in the determination of samples. As depicted in Fig. S3c, after adding a certain amount of H2O2, the fluorescence of N-CQDs/Fe3+/AA system was gradually turned off again and there is a good linear response (R2 = 0.996) when the concentration of H2O2 is 40–140 μM with the regression equation of F = -1.55 CH2O2 (μM) + 509.25 (Fig. S3d). The calculated detection limit is 8.86 μM (S/N = 3). The comparison results of different sensors for the detection of Fe3+, AA and H2O2 in recent years are summarized in Table S1, Table S2 and Table S3, respectively. Compared with reported references, although the detection limit is not very low and the linear range is not very wide, the continuous response for three small molecules is very fascinating and interesting. Moreover, we find that the linear range of H2O2 is slightly narrower than that of Fe3+ and AA. This may be because H2O2, as the end of the continuous response fluorescence sensor, has a slightly poor response sensitivity. However, it is still interesting because it can achieve continuous response. Efforts to improve the linear range of H2O2 can be taken as a direction of subsequent research.

In order to check the practical performance of the prepared fluorescence nano sensors, lake water, Sprite and tap water (Yantai, China) were chosen as the real samples for the determination of Fe3+, AA and H2O2 by standard addition recovery method and compared with standard atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) or high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) method. Different known concentration of Fe3+, AA or H2O2 was added to the diluted lake water (5%), sprite (5%) and tap water (5%) respectively, then mixed with N-CQDs, N-CQDs/Fe3+ or N-CQDs/Fe3+/AA and 0.05 M HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 4.0). Fluorescence intensity was determined by the proposed experimental method. As indicated in Table S4, the recoveries were in the range of 95.35% −104.10% with the relative standard deviation (RSD) ≤ 1.68%. As a contrast, the water samples were verified by AAS or HPLC method, and the results showed that there was no significant change between this method and AAS (or HPLC) method. These results clearly indicated that the prepared sensors were reliable and sensitive for the determination of Fe3+, AA and H2O2 in the environment and food fields.

We know that chemical experiments are often used to simulate computer circuit calculation [51]. Based on the above discussion, the logic gate was constructed successfully. First, three inputs were Fe3+ (input 1), AA (input 2) and H2O2 (input 3), and the presence of them was set as “1″ and the absence was set as ”0″. For the output, the normal fluorescence intensity was set to “1″ and the quenched fluorescence was set to ”0″. When both Fe3+ and AA were added as inputs, Fe3+ is reduced by AA to Fe2+ with low coordination ability, so the quenched fluorescence was restored and the “AND” logic gate was activated. Second, the fluorescence was quenched again when all inputs [Fe3+ = 1, AA = 1, H2O2 = 1] are given (Table S5) and the three inputs “NOT” logic gate was formed (Scheme 2 ) [52]. The various outputs for different input combinations were shown in Fig. S4.

The prepared N-CQDs can be used as a fluorescent ink because of its excellent fluorescence properties. The comparison of letters written on filter paper using N-CQDs solution under sunlight (Fig. 7 a) and ultraviolet (Fig. 7b) was shown in Fig. 7. At present, fluorescent ink is being popularized for printing purposes, and may be used in information transmission fields such as information storage and encryption in the future.

In this work, a green N-CQDs with good optical performance was prepared by the simple one-step hydrothermal synthesis method using the leaves of prunus lannesiana as both carbon source and nitrogen source. Through the continuous quenching and recovery of fluorescence intensity by Fe3+, AA and H2O2, the three inputs “logic gate” was operated and the “on-off-on-off” continuous response fluorescence sensor was formed. At the same time, a simple and rapid method was established, which could be successfully applied to the detection of Fe3+, AA and H2O2 in real samples. The obtained fluorescent probe with acceptable sensitivity and good selectivity has great prospect, which may be implicated in sensors and molecular computing [51,53].

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Key Research and Development Program of Shandong Province, China (2019GSF111047) and the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (ZR2020MB075, ZR2019MB019).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.119774.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments
J. Am. Chem., Soc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.Y. Xu, R. Ray, Y.L. Gu, H.J. Ploehn, L. Gearheart, K. Raker, W.A. Scrivens, Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments, J. Am. Chem., Soc. 126 (2004) 12736-12737.
